entries.title: John 1 · The Word Made Flesh
paragraphs: The evangelist begins not with the genealogy of Christ as a man, tracing his descent from Abraham or Adam, but with his eternal subsistence as the Word — going further back than any other gospel writer dares to go, even to the very beginning, before time was.

That the Word was in the beginning supposes his pre-existence before all creation. That the Word was with God establishes both his distinct personality and his eternal communion with the Father. That the Word was God declares his divine nature without qualification or hesitation.

The prologue moves with majestic inevitability toward its climax: the Word became flesh. Here is the miracle of the Incarnation — not that God spoke from heaven, but that God took on human nature, pitched his tent among us (for tabernacled is the word), and was seen in human form.

entries.title: Verse 14 — The Word Became Flesh
paragraphs: And the Word was made flesh — not merely appeared in the form of flesh, as some have taught, but actually became flesh: took a real human nature, body and soul, into personal union with himself. He who fills all things was contained in a manger.

We beheld his glory — the apostles speak of themselves as eyewitnesses. The glory they beheld was not the blinding glory of Sinai but the glory of grace: the glory of the only-begotten Son, which shone through his works, his words, and ultimately through his suffering and resurrection.

entries.title: John 3 · The New Birth and God's Love
paragraphs: This chapter contains two of the most important passages in the entire gospel: the conversation with Nicodemus on the new birth, and what many consider the gospel in miniature — John 3:16. Together they give us the nature and the ground of salvation.

Nicodemus comes to Jesus by night — whether from fear, or from a desire for unhurried conversation, or both, we cannot be certain. What is certain is that he comes as a seeker, acknowledging signs he cannot explain, reaching after a teacher sent from God.

The answer Jesus gives him leaps far beyond his question. He does not discuss signs; he declares the necessity of regeneration. You must be born again. This is not a reform of the old nature but the creation of a new one — not improvement but resurrection.

entries.title: Verse 16 — God So Loved the World
paragraphs: Here is the gospel compressed into a single sentence. For God so loved the world — not merely the elect, not merely Israel, but the world: the whole race of fallen, rebellious, perishing humanity. The love is particular in its application but universal in its offer.

That he gave his only-begotten Son — the gift measures the love. He did not give an angel, did not give a servant; he gave the one who was dearest to him, his only Son, whom he loved from before the foundation of the world.

That whosoever believes in him should not perish but have everlasting life — the condition is faith, the promise is life, and both are as wide as the word 'whosoever.' None are excluded by nature; only by unbelief.

entries.title: John 4 · Christ and the Samaritan Woman
paragraphs: Christ's coming into Samaria was not accidental but providential. He who wearied himself with a long journey did so that he might have opportunity to do good — revealing the condescending grace with which he sought those who were lost and knew it not.

The location is significant. Jacob's Well at Sychar connects this encounter to the patriarchal narratives of Genesis. John is building on the deep grammar of the Old Testament, where a well is always a place of meeting, of covenant encounter — Abraham's servant and Rebekah, Jacob and Rachel, Moses and Zipporah. Christ comes to fulfill what those stories only prefigured.

The woman of Samaria is met here not as a representative of respectability, but of need. That she comes at the sixth hour — midday, the heat of the day — rather than in the cool of the morning with the other women suggests one who has been excluded from the ordinary fellowship of her community. And it is precisely to such that Christ makes himself known.

entries.title: Verse 10 — The Gift of Living Water
paragraphs: The gift of God here is Christ himself, or rather the Spirit which he gives, and which is here called living water — distinguished from standing or stagnant water by the very fact that it moves, it rises, it refreshes. The contrast with Jacob's Well is deliberate: that well required labor, a bucket, and a rope; Christ's gift requires nothing but an open hand.

If you knew the gift of God — here is the root of all spiritual poverty, this not-knowing. The woman stands before the fountain of all grace and asks him for water. So too with us: we beg the creatures for what only the Creator can give.

That the water would become in the recipient a spring of water welling up to eternal life is a remarkable promise — not merely a supply from outside, but an indwelling, self-renewing principle of spiritual life. Augustine observed that this water does not satisfy thirst and then cease; it transforms the thirsty soul into a fountain.

entries.title: Verses 23–24 — Worship in Spirit and Truth
paragraphs: The woman raises the great controversy of her day — the question of the right place of worship. Christ's answer dissolves the question by declaring that the hour has come when neither Gerizim nor Jerusalem will be the defining issue. True worship cannot be localized; it is defined by its character, not its coordinates.

God is Spirit — this great declaration establishes the principle. A spiritual being must be worshipped in a manner consistent with his nature: with the spirit of man, and with truth. Not in the form alone, but in the reality the form points to; not by the body's posture alone, but by the soul's engagement.

The Father seeks such worshippers — a remarkable word. The Father is not reluctant to be found; he is himself in pursuit of true worshippers. This should silence every excuse that trembles before the throne.

entries.title: John 6 · The Bread of Life
paragraphs: Chapter six stands at the center of John's gospel as a great theological statement about the nature of saving faith. The feeding of five thousand provides the occasion; the discourse on the Bread of Life provides the substance. Christ multiplies loaves for the body, then offers himself as bread for the soul.

The crowd follows him for loaves and is told to labor for the meat that endures. They ask for a sign, invoking Moses and the manna in the wilderness. Christ's reply is radical: Moses did not give you the true bread from heaven; my Father gives you the true bread, which is myself.

I am the Bread of Life — the first of the great 'I AM' sayings in this gospel. He who comes to me shall never hunger; he who believes in me shall never thirst. The hunger and thirst of the soul are real; Christ alone satisfies them.

entries.title: John 11 · The Resurrection of Lazarus
paragraphs: The raising of Lazarus is the seventh and greatest sign in John's gospel — the climactic miracle that precipitates the decision of the Sanhedrin to put Christ to death. John presents it with deliberate care: the delay, the sisters' grief, the tomb, the command, the resurrection.

The delay of Jesus when he hears of Lazarus's illness is not carelessness but purpose. He stays two days longer so that the miracle will be unmistakable — so that when he arrives, there can be no question of a merely apparent death or a premature burial. Four days in the tomb, by all reckoning, means death.

The encounter with Martha reveals the nature of resurrection faith. Martha confesses belief in a future resurrection, at the last day. Christ lifts her sights to the present: I am the resurrection and the life. The resurrection is not merely an event in the future; it is a person standing before her.

entries.title: Verse 25 — I Am the Resurrection and the Life
paragraphs: This is one of the most majestic declarations in the New Testament. I am the resurrection — not merely the agent of it, not merely its instrument, but its very principle and source. Life is not something Christ merely possesses and distributes; it is what he is.

He that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live — the body may die, but the believer's connection to the living Christ cannot be severed by death. And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die — the second death has no power over those who live in Christ.

entries.title: John 14 · Let Not Your Heart Be Troubled
paragraphs: These chapters (14-16) are among the most precious in the New Testament, spoken by Christ on the eve of his death in the upper room. They are the farewell address of the Son of God: words of consolation, instruction, and promise to those who are about to be left, for a season, as sheep without a shepherd.

Let not your heart be troubled — this is the keynote. In the very night of his betrayal, surrounded by the shadow of Gethsemane and Calvary, he speaks as though all is well. And so it is, for all things are in his hand.

I go to prepare a place for you is the ground of the comfort. Death is not an ending but a departure; the grave is not a prison but a door. Christ goes before his people not as one who abandons but as one who pioneers.

entries.title: Verse 6 — The Way, the Truth, and the Life
paragraphs: Thomas asks the right question, even if his faith falters in the asking: we know not where you are going, how can we know the way? Christ's answer is not a map but a person: I am the Way — the only access to the Father. I am the Truth — the full and final revelation of what God is and what man needs. I am the Life — the source and sustainer of all spiritual existence.

No man comes to the Father but by me — exclusive, absolute, without qualification or exception. This exclusivity is not narrowness but grace: it is the grace of a definite gospel over against the vague consolations of religion without Christ.

entries.title: John 19 · The Crucifixion
paragraphs: John's account of the passion is written by one who was there — an eyewitness account of unique solemnity and precision. He records the details that only an eyewitness would note: the title over the cross in three languages, the dividing of garments, the gift of the beloved disciple to Mary, the water and blood.

The crucifixion is presented not as a tragedy but as a triumph. It is finished — Christ's last word from the cross in John's account — is not the gasp of a dying man but the proclamation of a completed work. The Greek tetelestai was used in commercial documents to indicate that a debt had been paid in full.

The piercing of his side, with the immediate outflow of blood and water, carries a significance John presses upon his readers: he who saw it bears witness, and his testimony is true. Whether the blood and water point to the sacraments, or to the medical reality of a ruptured heart, or to the twin benefits of the atonement, John presents it as a fact of theological weight.

entries.title: John 20 · The Resurrection
paragraphs: John records the resurrection with the quiet authority of one who has been to the empty tomb and handled the evidence. The grave clothes lying folded, the linen cloth separately rolled up — these are not the details of legend but of memory.

Mary Magdalene stands at the center of the resurrection morning, weeping beside the empty tomb, mistaking the risen Lord for the gardener until he speaks her name. That single word — Mary — contains more than all the arguments for the resurrection. She knows his voice.

The evening appearance to the disciples is followed, a week later, by the appearance for Thomas's sake. Thomas's doubt is handled with extraordinary grace: Christ does not rebuke him, but meets him exactly at his point of need. Blessed are they who have not seen, and yet have believed — the beatitude that covers every generation of readers.

entries.title: John 21 · Beside the Sea of Tiberias
paragraphs: The closing chapter has the character of an appendix — it takes place after the main work of the gospel is done, returning to the ordinary geography of Galilee and the ordinary labor of fishing, as if to say that life goes on after the resurrection.

The miraculous catch of fish and the breakfast on the shore carry echoes of the earlier miracle (Luke 5) and speak of Christ's provision for his servants as they labor in the world. The fire of coals, the bread, the fish — these are simple things, and in their simplicity they are full of grace.

The restoration of Peter, asked three times whether he loves Christ (answering for each denial), is one of the most moving passages in the gospels. Christ does not ignore the failure; he heals it. And he heals it in the same currency in which it was incurred — thrice questioned, thrice forgiven, thrice commissioned.
